steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Ostrander et al. assert that the consensus view is flawed. The consensus claims the size-reducing IGF1 mutation developed only within the last 20,000 years. To support the assertion that this is wrong, a finding must place the specific size-influencing mutation before 20,000 years ago.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'IGF1 has been isolated in genetic material from fossils more than 20,000 years old of the red wolf and certain other species related to dogs.': isolating the IGF1 gene in other species does not show that the specific size-influencing mutation predates 20,000 years in dog ancestors. ✗
  • Choice B — 'An additional mutation related to IGF1 affects the development of characteristics other than body size in smaller dog breeds.': a different mutation affecting unrelated traits does not challenge the timeline for the size mutation. ✗
  • Choice C — 'The mutation related to IGF1 influences body size and is found in 53,000-year-old genetic material from fossils of Siberian wolves, an ancestor of dogs.': this directly places the body-size-influencing IGF1 mutation 53,000 years ago — well before the 20,000-year consensus threshold — in a dog ancestor, supporting the claim that the consensus is wrong. ✓
  • Choice D — 'One variant of IGF1 is found only in smaller dog breeds and appears to have emerged no more than 20,000 years ago.': this confirms the consensus timeline rather than challenging it. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
